title: How the dissociator part activates during moments of joy
author: Kalesh

metaDescription:

htmlContent:
The moment joy arrives, something inside me pulls back.

It's not dramatic. It's not loud. It's this quiet, almost polite retreat. Like a shadow folding itself into a corner. I'll be laughing with a friend, feeling the warmth of connection, the lightness of being present. And then, without warning, a switch flips. The laughter feels hollow. The warmth turns to static. I'm watching myself from across the room, wondering who that person is who was just laughing.

Here's the thing. This isn't a sign that something is wrong with me. It's a sign that something in me learned, a long time ago, that joy is dangerous. That joy attracts attention. That joy leaves you exposed. And that exposure, for a traumatized nervous system, feels like standing naked in a battlefield.

The part that does this, the one that steps in during moments of happiness, is what many therapists call a dissociator part. Richard Schwartz, the creator of Internal Family Systems (IFS), describes parts like these as protectors. They're not trying to ruin your fun. They're trying to keep you alive. And if you've lived through complex trauma, your nervous system learned that survival means staying small, staying invisible, staying numb.

Joy threatens that strategy. So the dissociator part activates. It pulls the emergency brake. It sends you into a fog. It makes you feel unreal, disconnected, like you're watching a movie of your own life. And in that moment, you're not broken. You're protected.

What the dissociator part actually does in your body

Think about what happens when you feel truly happy. Maybe it's a moment of genuine laughter. Maybe it's a compliment that lands. Maybe it's the feeling of being loved, seen, accepted. Your body starts to open. Your heart rate changes. Your breath deepens. You feel a sense of expansion, of aliveness.

For a traumatized nervous system, expansion is terrifying. Because expansion means vulnerability. It means you're not on guard. It means you're not scanning for threats. And if you grew up in an environment where threats were real and constant, dropping your guard feels like inviting disaster.

Stephen Porges, the researcher who developed Polyvagal Theory, would say that the dissociator part is engaging the dorsal vagal system. It's the oldest, most primitive part of your nervous system. It's the freeze response. It's the one that says, "If I can't fight and I can't flee, I'll shut down so the threat doesn't see me."

But here's the twist. The threat isn't external anymore. It's the joy itself. The dissociator part doesn't distinguish between a real predator and the feeling of being happy. To that part, both feel equally dangerous. Because happiness, in your history, might have been followed by punishment, rejection, or abandonment. The part learned that joy is a setup. A trap. A promise that will be broken.

So it activates. Not with malice. With the desperate love of a protector who has kept you alive through hell. It pulls you away from the joy before the joy can hurt you. And it does it so fast, so , that you might not even notice it happened until the feeling is gone and you're left wondering why you feel so empty.

Stay with me here. This is important. The dissociator part is not your enemy. It's not something to fight or get rid of. It's a part of you that has been doing an incredibly difficult job, often without any thanks, for years or decades. The goal isn't to silence it. The goal is to understand it, to thank it, and to slowly help it see that you're not in the same danger anymore.

One of the foundational texts on trauma recovery is Trauma and Recovery (paid link) by Judith Herman. She was one of the first to name how trauma fragments the self. Reading it helped me understand that my dissociator part wasn't a flaw. It was a predictable response to an unpredictable world.

Why the dissociator part doesn't trust good moments

Let me ask you something. Have you ever been having a genuinely good time, and then a thought pops up like, "This won't last"? Or, "Something bad is about to happen"? Or maybe you just feel suddenly numb, like the joy was never really there?

That's the dissociator part doing its job. It's scanning the horizon for the inevitable crash. Because in your history, the crash always came. The parent who was kind one moment and cruel the next. The friend who turned on you. The love that was conditional, that could be withdrawn at any moment.

Your nervous system learned that joy is a trap. So it built a protector whose job is to pull you out of joy before the trap springs. That protector is fast. It's efficient. And it's invisible. You don't see it coming. You just feel the aftermath. The flatness. The emptiness. The sense that something is wrong with you because you can't seem to hold onto happiness.

But nothing is wrong with you. You have a part that is doing its job. And that job was necessary once. It might not be necessary now, but the part doesn't know that yet. It's stuck in the past, still trying to protect you from dangers that no longer exist.

One resource that often resonates deeply with C-PTSD survivors is Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link) by Pete Walker. He writes about the emotional flashbacks that can hijack good moments. He names the inner critic that shuts down joy before it can grow. Reading his work helped me see that my dissociator part wasn't alone. It had allies. The inner critic. The exile. The firefighter. They all worked together to keep me small and safe.

Wild, right? The very parts that make you feel broken are actually the parts that kept you alive. They're not the problem. The problem is that they're still running old software in a new environment.

How to befriend the dissociator part instead of fighting it

So what do you do when the dissociator part shows up during a good moment? Do you push through? Do you ignore it? Do you try to force yourself to feel joy?

No. That never works. Pushing against a protector only makes it stronger. It's like telling someone who's scared of the dark that there's nothing to be afraid of. Their fear doesn't disappear. It just gets louder.

Instead, try this. When you notice the switch flip, when the joy starts to fade and the numbness creeps in, stop. Don't try to fix it. Just notice it. Say to yourself, "Oh, there's that part again. The one that's trying to protect me."

Notice what just happened in your body. Where do you feel the dissociation? Is it in your head, like a fog? In your chest, like a weight? In your legs, like they're disconnected from the ground? Just observe. Don't judge. Don't analyze. Just be curious.

Then, if you can, talk to the part. Internally. Gently. Say something like, "I see you. I know you're trying to keep me safe. Thank you. I'm okay right now. We're okay."

This might feel strange at first. It might feel fake. That's okay. The part doesn't need perfect words. It needs to feel seen. It needs to know that you're not trying to destroy it. That you're on its side.

A gentle introduction to parts work that many people appreciate is No Bad Parts (paid link) by Richard Schwartz. He makes the case that every part, even the ones that seem destructive, has a positive intention. The dissociator part's intention is to protect you from the pain of joy lost. That's not a bad part. That's a loyal part.

Over time, as you build trust with this part, it will start to relax. It will learn that you can handle joy. That joy doesn't always lead to pain. That you can feel happy and still be safe. But this takes time. It takes patience. And it takes a lot of small, repeated experiences of safety.

The slow work of letting joy land

I'm not going to tell you that this gets easy. It doesn't. I still have moments where the dissociator part activates during a good time. I still feel the fog roll in. I still feel the pull toward numbness.

But the difference now is that I know what's happening. I can name it. I can say, "Oh, there you are. I see you. Thank you for trying to protect me. But I'm going to stay here for a moment. You can come with me if you want."

And sometimes, the part does come with me. It stays in the background, tense and watchful, but it doesn't pull me away. I get to keep a sliver of the joy. And that sliver, over time, becomes a little bigger. A little more solid. A little more real.

This is the slow work of recovery. It's not about forcing yourself to feel happy. It's about making space for the parts that are afraid of happiness. It's about letting them know that you're not going to abandon them. That you're not going to pretend they don't exist. That you're going to bring them along, gently, into the light.

A practical guide to boundaries that many find accessible is Set Boundaries, Find Peace (paid link) by Nedra Glover Tawwab. Boundaries aren't just about saying no to people. They're also about setting boundaries with your own parts. Letting the dissociator part know that it can take a step back. That you've got this. That joy is allowed.

So if you're reading this and you recognize yourself, if you've felt that pull away from joy, know that you're not alone. You're not broken. You have a part that has been doing a hard job for a long time. And that part deserves your compassion, not your contempt.

Sit with that. Let it land. And the next time joy shows up and the dissociator part activates, just notice. Just breathe. Just say, "I see you." That's enough. That's more than enough.

Healing Journey Resources

  • Trauma and Recovery (paid link) by Judith Herman
  • Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link) by Pete Walker
  • No Bad Parts (paid link) by Richard Schwartz
  • Set Boundaries, Find Peace (paid link) by Nedra Glover Tawwab
